description: Deep reference for core data structures — covers operations, variants, traps, and disqualifiers for each structure family.
usage: Use when two candidate paths share the same algorithmic paradigm. The data structure chosen determines the constants, access patterns, mutation cost, and feasibility.

families.name: Heap / Priority Queue
families.coreContract: Insert in O(log n); extract the minimum (or maximum) in O(log n); peek in O(1). Does not support arbitrary access or update without augmentation.
whenToPrefer: You repeatedly need the globally best element (min or max) from a changing set
Dijkstra-style 'process cheapest frontier node next'
K-way merging, top-K queries, sliding window maximum without monotonic queue

variants.name: Binary min/max heap
variants.characteristic: Standard; O(log n) push/pop
variants.bestUsedWhen: General priority queue needs

variants.name: Indexed heap (heap + position map)
variants.characteristic: O(log n) decrease-key by tracking positions
variants.bestUsedWhen: Dijkstra with edge relaxation, Prim's MST

variants.name: Two-heap partition
variants.characteristic: One min-heap + one max-heap sharing a population
variants.bestUsedWhen: Median of a data stream, range median

variants.name: Monotonic deque (deque-as-heap)
variants.characteristic: Maintains sorted order by evicting dominated elements
variants.bestUsedWhen: Sliding window max/min in O(n) total

variants.name: Lazy deletion heap
variants.characteristic: Stale entries left in heap; validity checked at pop time
variants.bestUsedWhen: Interval scheduling, task cooldown, sweep line events

variants.name: K-way merge heap
variants.characteristic: One entry per input stream; pop + re-insert from same stream
variants.bestUsedWhen: Merge K sorted lists, external sort

implementationTraps: Python's heapq is min-heap only — negate values for max-heap; don't forget to negate back
Lazy deletion requires a 'seen' set or a version counter; forgetting this causes incorrect pops
Two-heap median: rebalancing invariant (size difference <= 1) must be maintained on every insert and remove
Indexed heap position map must be updated on every swap during sift-up/sift-down, not just at insert
disqualifiers: Need to find an arbitrary element (use hash map instead)
Need sorted iteration over the full set (use BST or sort)
The set is static — just sort once

families.name: Tree Variants
families.coreContract: A family of hierarchical structures offering O(log n) search, insert, and delete on ordered data, plus range queries. The specific variant determines what additional operations are O(log n) vs O(n).
whenToPrefer: Data is ordered and you need both point queries and range queries
Dynamic set with frequent insertions/deletions (vs. static -> just sort)
Need rank queries ('how many elements <= X?') or order statistics

variants.name: BST (unbalanced)
variants.characteristic: Simple; degrades to O(n) on sorted input
variants.bestUsedWhen: Never in production; useful for teaching

variants.name: AVL / Red-Black tree
variants.characteristic: Self-balancing; O(log n) guaranteed
variants.bestUsedWhen: Ordered map/set (most language std libs)

variants.name: Segment tree
variants.characteristic: Each node covers a range; supports range query + point update
variants.bestUsedWhen: Range sum/min/max with point updates

variants.name: Segment tree with lazy propagation
variants.characteristic: Deferred range updates; O(log n) range update + range query
variants.bestUsedWhen: Range add + range sum, range set + range min

variants.name: Fenwick tree (BIT)
variants.characteristic: Implicit tree via index bit tricks; smaller constant than segment tree
variants.bestUsedWhen: Prefix sum with point update; 2D variants for grid

variants.name: Trie (prefix tree)
variants.characteristic: Characters at edges; O(L) ops where L = string length
variants.bestUsedWhen: Prefix matching, autocomplete, XOR maximization

variants.name: Persistent segment tree
variants.characteristic: Each update creates a new root; old versions accessible
variants.bestUsedWhen: Range kth element, versioned data, offline queries

variants.name: Merge sort tree
variants.characteristic: Segment tree where each node stores a sorted array of its range
variants.bestUsedWhen: Count elements in range [L,R] satisfying a predicate

variants.name: K-d tree
variants.characteristic: Partitions k-dimensional space alternating on each axis
variants.bestUsedWhen: Nearest neighbor in 2D/3D, range search in space

variants.name: Cartesian tree / Treap
variants.characteristic: Heap-ordered by priority, BST-ordered by key; randomized balance
variants.bestUsedWhen: Implicit sequence with split/merge, rope data structure

implementationTraps: Segment tree: 1-indexed vs 0-indexed; size must be next power of 2 or use recursive build
Lazy propagation: the lazy tag must be pushed down before recursing into children, not after
Fenwick tree: update(i, delta) and query(i) are both 1-indexed; off-by-one errors are common
Trie: remember to handle end-of-word markers separately from the existence of a child node
Persistent structures: copying the path (O(log n) nodes) not the whole tree
disqualifiers: Updates are rare and queries are batch -> sort offline instead
Data is unordered and you only need exact-match lookup -> hash map is faster and simpler
N is small (< 1000) -> just use a sorted array with binary search

families.name: Hash-Based Structures
families.coreContract: O(1) average insert, delete, and lookup. No ordering. Worst-case O(n) due to collisions, but rare with good hash functions.
whenToPrefer: Fast exact-match lookup is the bottleneck
Counting frequencies, detecting duplicates, memoizing seen states
The key space is large but the active set is sparse

variants.name: HashMap / dictionary
variants.characteristic: Key -> value; O(1) average
variants.bestUsedWhen: Any frequency count, index lookup, memo table

variants.name: HashSet
variants.characteristic: Membership only; O(1) average
variants.bestUsedWhen: Visited set in BFS, duplicate detection

variants.name: Counting array
variants.characteristic: Fixed-range keys (e.g., chars, small ints); O(1) true worst-case
variants.bestUsedWhen: Character frequency, bucket sort

variants.name: Rolling hash
variants.characteristic: Hash of a sliding window updated incrementally
variants.bestUsedWhen: Rabin-Karp substring search, longest duplicate substring

variants.name: Polynomial hash / string hashing
variants.characteristic: Map strings to integers for O(1) comparison
variants.bestUsedWhen: Anagram grouping, palindrome detection over substrings

variants.name: Two-sum complement map
variants.characteristic: Store seen values; for each new value check if complement exists
variants.bestUsedWhen: Two-sum, subarray sum equals K (prefix sum + map)

variants.name: Coordinate compression + map
variants.characteristic: Map large sparse values to dense indices
variants.bestUsedWhen: Segment tree on large value range, offline range queries

implementationTraps: Rolling hash collisions: use double hashing (two independent moduli) to reduce false positives
Python dicts preserve insertion order (3.7+) — don't rely on this in language-agnostic solutions
Counting array bounds: ensure the array is sized for the full input range, not just what you've seen
Coordinate compression: compress after reading all input; queries must use the same mapping as updates
disqualifiers: Need to iterate in sorted order (use BST or sorted array)
Need range queries ('all keys between X and Y') -> hash maps can't do this
Adversarial inputs can cause hash collisions -> use randomized hash or tree map

families.name: Graph Representations
families.coreContract: Encodes vertices and edges; the representation chosen governs the cost of the operations most frequently used (adjacency test, neighbor enumeration, edge weight lookup).

variants.name: Adjacency list
variants.characteristic: Space O(V + E); adjacency test O(degree); neighbor list O(degree)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Sparse graphs; most real problems

variants.name: Adjacency matrix
variants.characteristic: Space O(V^2); adjacency test O(1); neighbor list O(V)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Dense graphs; Floyd-Warshall; small V

variants.name: Edge list
variants.characteristic: Space O(E); adjacency test O(E); neighbor list O(E)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Kruskal's MST; sorting edges by weight

variants.name: Implicit graph
variants.characteristic: Space O(1) storage; computed on-the-fly
variants.bestUsedWhen: BFS/DFS on grid, state-space search

variants.name: CSR (Compressed Sparse Row)
variants.characteristic: Space O(V + E); adjacency test O(log degree); neighbor list O(degree)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Read-only graphs; cache-efficient traversal

variants.name: Multigraph with edge ids
variants.characteristic: Space O(V + E); adjacency test O(degree); neighbor list O(degree)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Multiple edges between same nodes; flow networks

implicitGraphPatterns: Grid as graph: cell (r, c) is a node; 4/8 neighbors are edges
State as node: (position, remaining_fuel, keys_held) is a node in BFS
Interval as node: edge from interval A to B if A ends before B starts (interval scheduling graph)
Bitmask as node: each subset is a node; DP on subsets is BFS on hypercube graph
implementationTraps: Directed vs. undirected: adding edge u->v in an undirected graph requires also adding v->u
Self-loops and parallel edges: adjacency matrix handles them naturally; adjacency list needs care
Implicit graphs: ensure the neighbor-generation function is correct for boundary cells (grid edges)
Graph of intervals: when converting intervals to a DAG, ensure you sort by start time first
disqualifiers: Problem is fundamentally about ordered sequences, not relationships -> don't force a graph
V is huge but edges are implicit and acyclic -> DP on the implicit DAG is cleaner than building the graph

families.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
families.coreContract: Two operations — union(a, b) merges two sets; find(a) returns the canonical representative of a's set. With union-by-rank and path compression: both operations are effectively O(alpha(n)) ~ O(1).
whenToPrefer: Dynamic connectivity: 'are these two nodes in the same component after adding edges?'
Detecting cycles while building a spanning tree (Kruskal's)
Offline connectivity queries where edges only appear, never disappear

variants.name: Standard DSU
variants.characteristic: Union by rank + path compression
variants.bestUsedWhen: Component membership, Kruskal's MST

variants.name: Weighted/labeled DSU
variants.characteristic: Each node stores a value relative to its parent
variants.bestUsedWhen: Accounts with transfers, relative distances

variants.name: Rollback DSU (link-cut)
variants.characteristic: Union by rank without path compression; supports undo
variants.bestUsedWhen: Offline dynamic connectivity, divide-and-conquer on edges

variants.name: DSU on tree (small-to-large)
variants.characteristic: Merge smaller child's data into larger child's; O(n log n) total
variants.bestUsedWhen: Subtree queries, color counting on trees

variants.name: Bipartite checking DSU
variants.characteristic: Two nodes per vertex (true/false state); union checks consistency
variants.bestUsedWhen: 2-colorability, odd cycle detection

implementationTraps: Path compression alone (without union by rank) gives O(log n) not O(alpha(n))
Rollback DSU: path compression breaks rollback — must use union by rank only
Weighted DSU: when doing path compression, the weights along the path must be composed correctly
Off-by-one in initialization: parent[i] = i for all nodes, including isolated ones
disqualifiers: Edges can be removed as well as added -> DSU doesn't support deletions; use link-cut trees or offline processing
Need to enumerate all members of a component -> DSU only gives representative, not membership list
Need shortest path -> DSU knows connectivity but not distance

families.name: Monotonic Stack and Queue
families.coreContract: A stack or deque maintained in sorted order by evicting elements that can never be the answer to a future query. Each element is pushed and popped at most once -> O(n) total across all operations.
whenToPrefer: 'Next greater / smaller element' for each position
Sliding window maximum or minimum in O(n)
Largest rectangle / container problems
Problems where earlier elements become irrelevant once a better candidate appears

variants.name: Monotonic increasing stack
variants.characteristic: Bottom to top: increasing
variants.bestUsedWhen: Next smaller element, largest rectangle in histogram

variants.name: Monotonic decreasing stack
variants.characteristic: Bottom to top: decreasing
variants.bestUsedWhen: Next greater element, stock span problem

variants.name: Monotonic deque (sliding window)
variants.characteristic: Front is current best; rear evicts dominated elements
variants.bestUsedWhen: Sliding window max/min, maximum of all subarrays of size K

variants.name: Two-stack queue
variants.characteristic: Two stacks simulate O(1) amortized queue with min/max tracking
variants.bestUsedWhen: Min-queue (queue supporting O(1) minimum queries)

variants.name: Cartesian tree construction
variants.characteristic: Monotonic stack builds the Cartesian tree in O(n)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Range minimum query structure, treap-like problems

implementationTraps: Deciding what to store: often store indices, not values, so you can compute spans/widths
Equality handling: 'strictly greater' vs 'greater or equal' changes which duplicates survive
Sliding window deque: evict from the front when the window slides past an element, from the rear when a new element dominates
Off-by-one in the span calculation: right - left - 1 not right - left when both pointers are exclusive
disqualifiers: Window size changes non-monotonically (deque no longer sufficient; use segment tree)
Need arbitrary access into the 'stack' by value (use a BST instead)
Query isn't about a contiguous range or a sliding window

families.name: Sliding Window and Two Pointer
families.coreContract: Maintain a contiguous subarray (sliding window) or two indices moving toward each other (two pointer) to answer queries in O(n) rather than O(n^2).
whenToPrefer: 'Longest/shortest subarray satisfying a property'
'Pair of elements summing to a target in a sorted array'
Constraint on a subarray is monotone: expanding the window can only make it harder (or easier) to satisfy

variants.name: Fixed-size window
variants.characteristic: Right advances; left = right - k
variants.bestUsedWhen: Average of subarrays of size K, max sum of size K

variants.name: Variable-size window (shrink when invalid)
variants.characteristic: Right advances freely; left advances when constraint violated
variants.bestUsedWhen: Longest substring without repeating chars, min window substring

variants.name: Two pointer on sorted array
variants.characteristic: Left from start, right from end; move based on comparison
variants.bestUsedWhen: Two-sum in sorted array, container with most water

variants.name: Two pointer on linked list
variants.characteristic: Fast and slow pointers
variants.bestUsedWhen: Cycle detection, find middle node, Kth from end

variants.name: Multi-pointer (three pointers)
variants.characteristic: One anchor + two-pointer sweep
variants.bestUsedWhen: Three-sum, four-sum

variants.name: Prefix sum + two pointer
variants.characteristic: Precompute prefix sums; binary search or two-pointer on them
variants.bestUsedWhen: Subarray sum >= K (positive values), minimum size subarray sum

implementationTraps: Variable window: the shrink condition must restore validity fully before recording an answer
Two pointer on unsorted data: requires sorting first — the sort cost O(n log n) must be acceptable
Window with a frequency map: decrement the map when the left pointer moves; don't delete keys, check count == 0
Sliding window on circular arrays: run two passes or use modular indexing
disqualifiers: Constraint is not monotone (e.g., 'subarray with sum exactly K' on mixed-sign input -> prefix sum + hash map instead)
Array is unsorted and sorting would destroy required ordering
Queries are offline and non-contiguous (use segment tree or BIT)

families.name: Bit Manipulation and Bitsets
families.coreContract: Operate on integers as arrays of bits. Modern CPUs execute bitwise ops in O(1) on 64-bit words; bitsets compress boolean arrays by 64x, enabling O(n/64) set operations.
whenToPrefer: State is a subset of a small universe (N <= 20 for full bitmask DP; N <= 10^6 for bitset)
Need fast set intersection, union, or complement
Parity, power-of-two, or lowest-set-bit tricks replace arithmetic

variants.name: Bitmask DP
variants.characteristic: State = bitmask of chosen items; transition flips bits
variants.bestUsedWhen: TSP, set cover, assignment with N <= 20

variants.name: XOR trick
variants.characteristic: a ^ b ^ b = a; XOR of a pair cancels
variants.bestUsedWhen: Find single non-duplicate, missing number, swap without temp

variants.name: Lowest set bit (LSB)
variants.characteristic: x & (-x) isolates the rightmost 1
variants.bestUsedWhen: Fenwick tree indexing, iterating over set bits

variants.name: Popcount
variants.characteristic: bin(x).count('1') or hardware POPCNT
variants.bestUsedWhen: Hamming distance, count set bits, parity

variants.name: Bit shifting for powers
variants.bestUsedWhen: Fast multiply/divide by powers of 2, flag masks

variants.name: Bitset DP
variants.characteristic: Use Python int or C++ bitset<N> as a dense boolean array
variants.bestUsedWhen: Subset sum feasibility for large N, reachability in boolean matrix

variants.name: Gray code
variants.characteristic: Consecutive values differ by exactly one bit
variants.bestUsedWhen: Generating permutations/subsets without revisiting, rotary encoders

implementationTraps: Signed integer overflow: 1 << 31 is undefined in C++ with int; use 1LL << 31
Python integers are arbitrary precision — bitmask DP works for N > 64, but gets slow
XOR swap: a ^= b; b ^= a; a ^= b fails when a and b are the same variable (same memory)
Iterating over all subsets of a mask: sub = mask; while sub > 0: process(sub); sub = (sub-1) & mask — don't forget to process sub = 0 separately if needed
disqualifiers: N > 20 for bitmask DP (2^20 = 1M states is borderline; 2^25 is too large)
Operations require ordering or range queries (bits don't have positional meaning beyond index)
Problem involves arithmetic that doesn't reduce to bit tricks

families.name: Service Container / Dependency Injection
families.coreContract: Manage object creation and dependency resolution through a centralized registry. Consumers declare dependencies; the container provides concrete implementations at construction time or on demand.
whenToPrefer: Testability requires swapping real implementations for mocks or stubs
Loose coupling between modules with configurable dependency graphs
Multiple implementations of the same interface selected at runtime

variants.name: Constructor injection
variants.characteristic: Dependencies passed as constructor parameters; immutable after creation
variants.bestUsedWhen: Default choice for DI; clear dependency declaration, easy to test

variants.name: Property injection
variants.characteristic: Dependencies set via setters or public properties after construction
variants.bestUsedWhen: Optional dependencies, circular dependency resolution, framework-managed lifecycle

variants.name: Service locator
variants.characteristic: Global registry queried at runtime; consumers pull dependencies
variants.bestUsedWhen: Legacy codebases, plugin systems where consumers are unknown at compile time

variants.name: Factory registry
variants.characteristic: Registry maps keys to factory functions; creates instances on demand
variants.bestUsedWhen: Dynamic object creation, strategy selection, handler dispatch by type

implementationTraps: Service locator hides dependencies — call sites don't declare what they need, making refactoring and testing harder
Circular dependencies cause infinite loops or incomplete initialization; detect cycles at registration time
Singleton vs transient lifetime confusion: shared mutable state leaks between consumers if lifetime is wrong
Over-abstraction: injecting interfaces for every dependency when only one implementation will ever exist
disqualifiers: Simple scripts or utilities with no dependency graph
Performance-critical hot paths where indirection overhead matters
Projects with fewer than 3-4 modules — direct instantiation is simpler

families.name: Message Channel / Event Bus
families.coreContract: Decouple producers from consumers by routing messages through a shared channel. Publishers emit events without knowing who listens; subscribers react without knowing who published.
whenToPrefer: Decoupled communication between modules or processes
Async workflows where producer should not block on consumer
Cross-process or cross-service messaging

variants.name: In-process event emitter
variants.characteristic: Synchronous or microtask-based dispatch within a single process
variants.bestUsedWhen: Module decoupling in Node.js/browser, UI component communication

variants.name: IPC channel (Electron/Node)
variants.characteristic: Structured message passing between OS processes with serialization boundary
variants.bestUsedWhen: Electron main/renderer communication, Node child_process, worker threads

variants.name: Message broker (RabbitMQ/Redis pub-sub)
variants.characteristic: Network-based message routing with persistence, fan-out, and acknowledgment
variants.bestUsedWhen: Microservice communication, event-driven architecture, workload distribution

variants.name: Command bus (CQRS-style)
variants.characteristic: One-to-one dispatch: each command has exactly one handler
variants.bestUsedWhen: CQRS pattern, explicit command/query separation, audit trail

implementationTraps: Memory leaks from unremoved event listeners — always unsubscribe on teardown
Event ordering assumptions: async channels may deliver out of order; design for idempotency
Serialization boundary: IPC and broker channels require serializable payloads — no functions, circular refs, or class instances
Silent failures: events with no listeners vanish silently; add dead-letter or unhandled-event logging
disqualifiers: Synchronous function call is sufficient and simpler
Single-process application with no isolation or decoupling needs
Strict ordering and transactional guarantees required without additional infrastructure

families.name: Sandbox Environment / Test Isolation
families.coreContract: Create an isolated execution context where side effects (filesystem, environment, network, process state) are contained and do not leak into the host or other tests.
whenToPrefer: Integration or E2E tests that touch filesystem, network, or environment variables
CI pipelines requiring hermetic test execution
Test isolation to prevent cross-test contamination

variants.name: Temp directory isolation
variants.characteristic: Tests operate in an OS-provided temporary directory; cleaned up after each run
variants.bestUsedWhen: File I/O tests, config file generation, snapshot testing

variants.name: Virtual/mock filesystem
variants.characteristic: In-memory filesystem that intercepts fs calls without touching disk
variants.bestUsedWhen: Unit-level file tests, fast feedback loops, no disk cleanup needed

variants.name: Env var sandboxing
variants.characteristic: Save, override, and restore environment variables per test
variants.bestUsedWhen: Testing configuration layers, feature flag behavior, multi-environment logic

variants.name: Process fork with clean state
variants.characteristic: Spawn a child process with controlled env, args, and working directory
variants.bestUsedWhen: CLI tool testing, testing process lifecycle, signal handling

variants.name: Docker container isolation
variants.characteristic: Full OS-level isolation with reproducible image and network namespace
variants.bestUsedWhen: Database integration tests, multi-service E2E, environment parity with production

implementationTraps: Temp directory cleanup failures leave test artifacts on disk; use try/finally or afterEach hooks
Mock filesystem divergence from real fs behavior (permissions, symlinks, encoding) causes false positives
Env var restoration order matters when tests run in parallel — use per-test snapshots, not global save/restore
Docker startup latency can dominate test runtime; use persistent containers with state reset between tests
disqualifiers: Unit tests with mocks and stubs provide sufficient isolation
No file, process, or environment side effects in the code under test
Test runtime budget does not allow container or process startup overhead

families.name: Configuration Layer
families.coreContract: Externalize runtime settings from code into a layered, validated, environment-aware configuration system. Later layers override earlier ones; schemas catch invalid values before they reach application logic.
whenToPrefer: Multi-environment deployment (dev, staging, production) with different settings
Gradual feature rollout via feature flags
Validated settings that must conform to a known schema

variants.name: Environment variable cascade (dotenv)
variants.characteristic: Layered .env files with precedence: .env.local > .env.{NODE_ENV} > .env
variants.bestUsedWhen: 12-factor apps, container deployments, platform-provided config

variants.name: Feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash)
variants.characteristic: Remote flag evaluation with targeting rules, percentage rollouts, kill switches
variants.bestUsedWhen: Gradual rollouts, A/B testing, instant kill switch without deploy

variants.name: Schema-validated JSON/YAML config
variants.characteristic: Config files validated against JSON Schema or Zod/Yup at load time
variants.bestUsedWhen: Complex configuration with nested objects, type safety, documentation-as-schema

variants.name: Runtime config reload
variants.characteristic: Watch config source for changes; hot-reload without process restart
variants.bestUsedWhen: Long-running services, tuning parameters in production, feature flag sync

variants.name: Config inheritance (base + override)
variants.characteristic: Base config merged with environment-specific overrides using deep merge
variants.bestUsedWhen: Monorepo shared config, white-label apps, tenant-specific settings

implementationTraps: Env var type coercion: everything is a string — '0' is truthy in JS; parse explicitly
Secret leakage: config dumps or error messages may expose sensitive values; redact before logging
Override precedence confusion: document the merge order clearly; conflicting sources cause silent bugs
Schema drift: config schema and code expectations diverge over time; validate at startup, not just at build
disqualifiers: Single-environment application with no deployment variation
Hardcoded values are acceptable and unlikely to change
Configuration complexity exceeds the application complexity it serves

families.name: API Gateway / Edge Layer
families.coreContract: A single entry point that routes, transforms, authenticates, and rate-limits requests before they reach backend services. Clients see one API surface; the gateway handles cross-cutting concerns.
whenToPrefer: Multiple backend services behind a unified API surface
Auth consolidation: one place for token validation, API keys, OAuth
Rate limiting, request transformation, or response aggregation at the edge

variants.name: Reverse proxy (nginx, Envoy)
variants.characteristic: Path-based routing, TLS termination, load balancing; no application logic
variants.bestUsedWhen: Static routing, TLS offload, simple load balancing

variants.name: Backend-for-frontend (BFF)
variants.characteristic: Dedicated backend per client type (web, mobile, IoT) aggregating backend calls
variants.bestUsedWhen: Different clients need different data shapes, reduce over-fetching

variants.name: Edge function (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge)
variants.characteristic: Code runs at CDN edge; sub-millisecond cold start, geographic distribution
variants.bestUsedWhen: A/B testing at edge, geo-routing, auth token validation, header manipulation

variants.name: API gateway (Kong, AWS API Gateway)
variants.characteristic: Full-featured gateway with plugins for auth, rate limiting, transformation, observability
variants.bestUsedWhen: Enterprise API management, developer portal, quota enforcement, request/response transformation

implementationTraps: Single point of failure: gateway outage takes down all services; deploy with redundancy and health checks
Latency overhead: every request pays gateway processing cost; minimize middleware chain
Configuration sprawl: gateway routing rules become a second source of truth alongside service code
Over-aggregation in BFF: putting business logic in the gateway instead of backend services
disqualifiers: Single backend service with direct client access
Internal service-to-service communication that doesn't need edge concerns
Latency-sensitive paths where gateway overhead is unacceptable

families.name: Contract Definition / API-First Design
families.coreContract: Define the interface between systems as a machine-readable specification before implementation. The contract drives code generation, documentation, validation, and client SDKs.
whenToPrefer: API-first design where contract is agreed before implementation
Client code generation from a specification
Documentation-as-code with guaranteed accuracy
Multi-team contracts where producer and consumer develop in parallel

variants.name: OpenAPI/Swagger spec
variants.characteristic: YAML/JSON spec for REST APIs; drives codegen, docs, and validation middleware
variants.bestUsedWhen: REST APIs, broad tooling ecosystem, documentation portals

variants.name: GraphQL SDL + codegen
variants.characteristic: Schema Definition Language with type-safe client/server codegen
variants.bestUsedWhen: Flexible queries, strong typing, frontend-driven data requirements

variants.name: Protocol Buffers
variants.characteristic: Binary serialization format with strict schema evolution rules and gRPC integration
variants.bestUsedWhen: High-performance RPC, polyglot services, strict backward compatibility

variants.name: JSON Schema
variants.characteristic: Schema for JSON data structures; used for config validation, API payload validation
variants.bestUsedWhen: Validating request/response payloads, config files, event payloads

variants.name: AsyncAPI
variants.characteristic: OpenAPI-style spec for event-driven and message-based APIs
variants.bestUsedWhen: Message broker contracts, event-driven architecture documentation, async API portals

implementationTraps: Spec drift: implementation diverges from the contract; use CI validation (e.g., spectral lint, contract tests)
Over-specification: defining every possible field upfront; iterate with required fields first
Codegen lock-in: generated code becomes hard to customize; use extension points or partial generation
Version field omission: forgetting to version the contract makes breaking changes undetectable
disqualifiers: Internal-only API with a single consumer where direct function calls suffice
Rapid prototyping where the API shape changes every day
Overhead of maintaining the spec exceeds the coordination benefit

families.name: Index Strategy (Database)
families.coreContract: Create auxiliary data structures in the database that trade write-time overhead and storage for dramatically faster read queries. The right index turns a full table scan into an index lookup.
whenToPrefer: Query optimization for frequently executed read patterns
Specific access patterns (range scans, equality checks, full-text search, spatial queries)
Read-heavy workloads where index maintenance cost is acceptable

variants.name: B-tree (default, range queries)
variants.characteristic: Balanced tree supporting equality and range predicates; default index type in most RDBMS
variants.bestUsedWhen: General-purpose indexing, ORDER BY, BETWEEN, >, <, = queries

variants.name: Hash index (equality only)
variants.characteristic: O(1) average lookup for exact match; no range support
variants.bestUsedWhen: Exact-match lookups on high-cardinality columns, in-memory tables

variants.name: GIN (full-text, array containment)
variants.characteristic: Generalized Inverted Index; indexes composite values (arrays, JSONB, tsvector)
variants.bestUsedWhen: Full-text search, JSONB containment queries, array overlap/contains

variants.name: GiST (geometric, spatial)
variants.characteristic: Generalized Search Tree; supports spatial, range, and nearest-neighbor queries
variants.bestUsedWhen: PostGIS geometry, range types, nearest-neighbor search

variants.name: Partial index (filtered subset)
variants.characteristic: Index only rows matching a WHERE predicate; smaller and faster than full index
variants.bestUsedWhen: Indexing active records (WHERE deleted_at IS NULL), rare status values

variants.name: Covering index (index-only scan)
variants.characteristic: Index includes all columns needed by the query; avoids table heap lookup
variants.bestUsedWhen: High-frequency queries where eliminating heap access matters

implementationTraps: Over-indexing: each index slows writes and consumes storage; benchmark before adding
Index not used: query planner may ignore an index due to low selectivity, type mismatch, or function wrapping the column
Composite index column order matters: (a, b) supports WHERE a = ? AND b = ? but not WHERE b = ? alone
Partial index predicate mismatch: the query WHERE clause must match the index predicate exactly for the planner to use it
disqualifiers: Write-heavy tables where index maintenance cost dominates read benefit
Tiny tables (< 1000 rows) where sequential scan is faster than index overhead
Columns with very low cardinality (boolean flags) unless used as partial index predicate

families.name: Migration Pipeline / Schema Evolution
families.coreContract: Apply versioned, ordered, and reversible changes to a database schema or data model. Each migration is a discrete step with an up (apply) and optionally a down (rollback) operation.
whenToPrefer: Schema evolution in production databases
Zero-downtime deployments requiring backward-compatible schema changes
Data transformation or backfill as part of a release

variants.name: Sequential versioned migrations (Flyway/Knex)
variants.characteristic: Numbered or timestamped scripts executed in order; tracks applied versions in a metadata table
variants.bestUsedWhen: Standard schema evolution, most web applications, team-based development

variants.name: Idempotent migration scripts
variants.characteristic: Scripts use IF NOT EXISTS / IF EXISTS guards; safe to re-run
variants.bestUsedWhen: Environments where migration state tracking is unreliable, disaster recovery

variants.name: Expand-contract pattern
variants.characteristic: Add new column/table (expand), migrate data, remove old (contract) across multiple deploys
variants.bestUsedWhen: Zero-downtime deployments, renaming columns, changing types

variants.name: Blue-green schema migration
variants.characteristic: Maintain two schema versions simultaneously; switch traffic after validation
variants.bestUsedWhen: High-risk schema changes, large data migrations, rollback-critical deployments

variants.name: Shadow table approach
variants.characteristic: Create new table with desired schema, dual-write, backfill, then swap
variants.bestUsedWhen: Large table restructuring, adding indexes on huge tables without locking

implementationTraps: Irreversible migrations: some operations (DROP COLUMN, data truncation) cannot be rolled back; plan data preservation
Migration ordering conflicts in team development: use timestamps instead of sequential numbers
Long-running migrations lock tables: use online DDL (pt-online-schema-change, gh-ost) for large tables
Data migration mixed with schema migration: separate structure changes from data backfills for easier debugging
disqualifiers: Schema never changes after initial creation
Development-only database that can be recreated from scratch
NoSQL databases where schema is implicit (though data migrations may still apply)

families.name: Distributed Lock / Coordination Primitive
families.coreContract: Ensure mutual exclusion across multiple processes or nodes. Only one holder can acquire the lock at a time; others block or fail fast. Locks must have TTL to prevent deadlocks from crashed holders.
whenToPrefer: Distributed coordination where only one process should perform an action
Resource exclusivity: preventing double-processing of a job or message
Leader election in a cluster

variants.name: Redis SETNX lock (Redlock)
variants.characteristic: Atomic set-if-not-exists with TTL; Redlock extends to multiple Redis instances for fault tolerance
variants.bestUsedWhen: Low-latency locking, Redis already in stack, short-lived locks

variants.name: ZooKeeper ephemeral node
variants.characteristic: Lock represented as ephemeral znode; automatically released if holder's session expires
variants.bestUsedWhen: Strong consistency requirements, leader election, configuration management

variants.name: Database advisory lock
variants.characteristic: Database-native lock (pg_advisory_lock, GET_LOCK) using existing database connection
variants.bestUsedWhen: No additional infrastructure needed, database already in stack, transaction-scoped locking

variants.name: etcd lease-based lock
variants.characteristic: Lock tied to a lease with TTL; revocation via lease expiry or explicit release
variants.bestUsedWhen: Kubernetes-native environments, strong consistency, watch-based coordination

variants.name: Optimistic locking (version column)
variants.characteristic: No explicit lock; read version, write with WHERE version = N, retry on conflict
variants.bestUsedWhen: Low contention scenarios, database-only coordination, no external lock service

implementationTraps: Clock skew in Redlock: nodes with drifting clocks can grant overlapping locks; use fencing tokens
Lock holder dies without releasing: always use TTL; design for lock expiry during normal operation
Retry storms: many clients retrying a contested lock simultaneously; use exponential backoff with jitter
Fencing token omission: downstream resources must validate the fencing token to prevent stale lock holders from acting
disqualifiers: Single-process application where in-memory mutex or semaphore suffices
Optimistic concurrency control (version column) is sufficient for the contention level
The coordination problem can be solved by partitioning work instead of locking

families.name: Message Queue / Delivery Semantics
families.coreContract: Asynchronously deliver messages from producers to consumers with defined delivery guarantees. The queue decouples production rate from consumption rate and provides buffering, retry, and ordering semantics.
whenToPrefer: Async processing where producer should not wait for consumer to finish
Workload distribution across multiple consumer instances
Retry semantics matter: at-least-once or exactly-once delivery required

variants.name: Fire-and-forget (at-most-once)
variants.characteristic: Message sent without acknowledgment; may be lost but never duplicated
variants.bestUsedWhen: Metrics, telemetry, non-critical notifications where loss is acceptable

variants.name: Acknowledged delivery (at-least-once)
variants.characteristic: Consumer acknowledges processing; unacknowledged messages are redelivered
variants.bestUsedWhen: Default for most workloads; idempotent consumers handle duplicates

variants.name: Transactional outbox (exactly-once)
variants.characteristic: Write message to outbox table in same DB transaction as state change; relay to queue separately
variants.bestUsedWhen: Financial transactions, order processing, any state change that must not be lost or duplicated

variants.name: Dead letter queue
variants.characteristic: Messages that fail processing after N retries are routed to a separate queue for inspection
variants.bestUsedWhen: Poison message handling, debugging failed processing, compliance audit trail

variants.name: Priority queue
variants.characteristic: Messages assigned priority levels; higher-priority messages processed first
variants.bestUsedWhen: SLA-differentiated processing, urgent vs batch workloads, tiered service

implementationTraps: At-least-once without idempotency: duplicate processing causes double charges, double emails, duplicate records
Message ordering assumptions: most queues do not guarantee order across partitions; use partition keys for ordered delivery
Consumer lag monitoring: unmonitored queue depth leads to unbounded growth and eventual OOM or disk exhaustion
Retry without backoff: immediate retry of a failing message creates a tight loop consuming resources
disqualifiers: Synchronous RPC is sufficient and simpler for the use case
Message ordering is critical but the queue cannot guarantee it
The system has a single producer and single consumer with no buffering needs

families.name: State Store / Client State Management
families.coreContract: Centralize or distribute application state in a predictable, observable container. State changes flow through defined pathways, enabling debugging, persistence, and reactive UI updates.
whenToPrefer: Shared UI state accessed by multiple components or routes
Server data synchronization with caching, invalidation, and optimistic updates
Reactive updates where state changes automatically propagate to dependent views

variants.name: Global store (Redux, Zustand)
variants.characteristic: Single store with actions/reducers; predictable state transitions, devtools, middleware
variants.bestUsedWhen: Complex shared state, time-travel debugging, middleware for side effects

variants.name: Atomic state (Jotai, Recoil)
variants.characteristic: State split into independent atoms; components subscribe to only what they read
variants.bestUsedWhen: Fine-grained reactivity, avoiding unnecessary re-renders, bottom-up state design

variants.name: Signals/observables (Solid, RxJS)
variants.characteristic: Reactive primitives with automatic dependency tracking; synchronous fine-grained updates
variants.bestUsedWhen: High-frequency updates, derived state computation, real-time data streams

variants.name: Server state cache (TanStack Query, SWR)
variants.characteristic: Cached server responses with automatic refetch, stale-while-revalidate, and optimistic updates
variants.bestUsedWhen: API data fetching, server state synchronization, pagination, infinite scroll

variants.name: URL state (search params)
variants.characteristic: State encoded in URL query parameters; shareable, bookmarkable, history-integrated
variants.bestUsedWhen: Filter/sort state, pagination, shareable views, deep linking

implementationTraps: Global store over-use: putting everything in global state when local component state suffices; causes unnecessary re-renders
Stale closure bugs: event handlers capturing outdated state references in React; use refs or functional updates
Server state vs client state confusion: mixing fetched data with UI state in the same store; use separate layers
Selector instability: selectors returning new object references on every call trigger unnecessary re-renders; memoize selectors
disqualifiers: Local component state (useState/useReducer) is sufficient for the use case
Static pages or server-rendered content with no client-side interactivity
Simple forms where form state libraries (React Hook Form, Formik) are more appropriate

families.name: Telemetry Pipeline / Observability Stack
families.coreContract: Collect, process, store, and visualize signals (logs, metrics, traces) from running systems. The pipeline transforms raw telemetry into actionable insights for debugging, alerting, and capacity planning.
whenToPrefer: Production systems requiring monitoring, alerting, and incident response
SLO tracking and reliability engineering
Distributed systems where request tracing across services is essential

variants.name: OpenTelemetry collector
variants.characteristic: Vendor-neutral telemetry collection with receivers, processors, and exporters
variants.bestUsedWhen: Multi-vendor flexibility, standardized instrumentation, polyglot services

variants.name: Log aggregator (Fluentd, Logstash)
variants.characteristic: Collect, parse, transform, and route logs from multiple sources to storage backends
variants.bestUsedWhen: Centralized logging, log parsing and enrichment, compliance retention

variants.name: Time-series DB (Prometheus, InfluxDB)
variants.characteristic: Optimized for high-write, time-indexed numeric data with downsampling and retention policies
variants.bestUsedWhen: Metrics storage, alerting rules, Grafana dashboards, capacity planning

variants.name: Visualization (Grafana, Datadog)
variants.characteristic: Dashboards, alerting, and exploration across multiple telemetry backends
variants.bestUsedWhen: Operational dashboards, SLO visualization, incident investigation

variants.name: APM (New Relic, Dynatrace)
variants.characteristic: Full-stack application performance monitoring with auto-instrumentation and AI-assisted root cause analysis
variants.bestUsedWhen: End-to-end transaction tracing, performance bottleneck identification, business-impact correlation

implementationTraps: Cardinality explosion: high-cardinality labels (user ID, request ID) on metrics cause storage and query cost to explode
Alert fatigue: too many noisy alerts lead to ignored pages; use SLO-based alerting with burn-rate windows
Missing context in logs: structured logging (JSON) with correlation IDs is essential; free-text logs are unsearchable at scale
Sampling blind spots: aggressive sampling may drop the traces you need most — keep 100% sampling for errors and slow requests
disqualifiers: Development-only or local-only applications
Printf debugging is sufficient for the project's complexity
Cost of telemetry infrastructure exceeds the value of the insights it provides

families.name: Credential Store / Secrets Management
families.coreContract: Store, access, rotate, and audit sensitive values (API keys, passwords, certificates) separately from application code. Secrets are never committed to version control or logged in plaintext.
whenToPrefer: Production secrets that must be rotated, audited, or access-controlled
Credential rotation requirements (compliance, security policy)
Multi-environment secret management with different values per environment

variants.name: HashiCorp Vault
variants.characteristic: Full-featured secrets engine with dynamic secrets, leasing, rotation, and fine-grained ACL
variants.bestUsedWhen: Enterprise secret management, dynamic database credentials, PKI certificate issuance

variants.name: Cloud secret manager (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager)
variants.characteristic: Managed service integrated with cloud IAM; automatic rotation for supported services
variants.bestUsedWhen: Cloud-native applications, IAM-based access control, managed rotation

variants.name: Encrypted config file
variants.characteristic: Secrets encrypted at rest in version control (SOPS, git-crypt); decrypted at deploy time
variants.bestUsedWhen: GitOps workflows, small teams, secrets-in-repo with encryption

variants.name: Environment variables
variants.characteristic: Secrets injected via env vars at runtime; no encryption at rest in the process
variants.bestUsedWhen: 12-factor apps, container orchestration, platform-injected secrets (Heroku, Render)

variants.name: Hardware security module (HSM)
variants.characteristic: Tamper-resistant hardware for key storage; keys never leave the device
variants.bestUsedWhen: Regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS, FIPS 140-2), root CA key storage, signing operations

implementationTraps: Secret sprawl: secrets duplicated across env vars, config files, and vault; single source of truth is essential
Logging secrets: structured logging or error handlers may serialize and log secret values; redact before logging
Rotation downtime: rotating a secret without updating all consumers simultaneously causes auth failures; use grace periods
Overly broad access: granting all services access to all secrets; use least-privilege policies per service
disqualifiers: Development-only project with no sensitive credentials
Public configuration values that are not secret
The overhead of secret management exceeds the risk (single-developer hobby project)

families.name: Orchestration Layer / Container Management
families.coreContract: Automate deployment, scaling, networking, and lifecycle management of containerized or serverless workloads. The orchestrator ensures desired state matches actual state across the cluster.
whenToPrefer: Multi-container applications requiring coordinated deployment
Auto-scaling based on load, schedule, or custom metrics
Multi-service deployment with service discovery and load balancing

variants.name: Kubernetes (k8s)
variants.characteristic: Full-featured container orchestration with declarative config, extensibility via CRDs, and massive ecosystem
variants.bestUsedWhen: Large-scale production deployments, multi-team platform, complex networking and storage needs

variants.name: AWS ECS/Fargate
variants.characteristic: AWS-native container orchestration; Fargate removes server management entirely
variants.bestUsedWhen: AWS-centric workloads, simpler operational model than k8s, serverless containers

variants.name: Serverless platform (Lambda, Cloud Functions)
variants.characteristic: Event-driven function execution; zero idle cost, automatic scaling, no server management
variants.bestUsedWhen: Event-driven workloads, API endpoints, scheduled tasks, low-traffic or bursty traffic

variants.name: Nomad
variants.characteristic: HashiCorp orchestrator supporting containers, VMs, and standalone binaries; simpler than k8s
variants.bestUsedWhen: Mixed workloads (containers + legacy), simpler operational model, HashiCorp ecosystem

variants.name: Docker Compose (dev/small-scale)
variants.characteristic: Multi-container definition in a single YAML file; local orchestration with docker-compose up
variants.bestUsedWhen: Local development, small-scale production, demo environments, CI test environments

implementationTraps: Kubernetes complexity tax: k8s operational overhead is significant; don't adopt it for a single service
Cold start latency: serverless functions have variable cold start times; pre-warming or provisioned concurrency may be needed
Resource limit misconfiguration: too-tight CPU/memory limits cause OOM kills and throttling; too-loose wastes money
Service discovery assumptions: hardcoded hostnames break when orchestrators reassign IPs; use DNS-based service discovery
disqualifiers: Single server deployment where a process manager (systemd, PM2) suffices
Static hosting (CDN/S3) for frontend-only applications
The team does not have the operational expertise to run the chosen orchestrator

crossStructureDecisionGuide.bottleneckOperation: Range query + point update on array
Exact membership / frequency of arbitrary keys
Sliding window aggregate in linear time
Test isolation from filesystem, env, or process side effects
Unified API surface over multiple backends
Machine-readable API contract for codegen and validation
Slow read queries on large tables
Async workload distribution with retry semantics
Shared reactive UI state across components
Secure storage and rotation of production secrets
Multi-container deployment with scaling and service discovery
